name: Haya
description: The Haya inhabit the Kagera Region on the western shore of Lake Victoria — a lush, banana-growing landscape that supported multiple small kingdoms (Karagwe, Kiziba, Bugabo, Ihangiro, Kyamutwara). The Haya developed one of Africa's most sophisticated pre-industrial technologies: high-carbon steel smelting using forced-air blast furnaces around 1500 years ago — centuries before European steel-making achieved similar results. This discovery, documented by archaeologist Peter Schmidt, overturned long-held assumptions about African technological development.
cultural_highlights: Ancient steel smelting — the Haya operated blast furnace steel production c. 1500–2000 years ago, producing carbon steel in preheated forced-air furnaces centuries before Europe achieved the same
Multiple kingdom system — the Haya maintained eight separate kingdoms simultaneously, each with distinct royal clans, musical traditions, and governance
Coffee culture — the Haya have cultivated Robusta coffee for centuries; chewing coffee beans as a social gift predates the Ottoman coffee house by generations
Banana civilisation — over 100 varieties of banana cultivated for food, beer, medicine, and construction material

food_traditions.name: Omwenge (banana beer)
food_traditions.description: Fermented banana beer — the daily drink of Haya society; offered to guests and ancestors alike

food_traditions.name: Matoke (boiled banana)
food_traditions.description: Steamed highland cooking banana — the daily staple across all Haya kingdoms

food_traditions.name: Ekitooke (roasted banana)
food_traditions.description: Roasted ripe banana eaten as a snack or accompaniment to stews

music_traditions: Amakondere — curved ivory and animal horn trumpets used in royal court music of the Haya kingdoms
Ntongoli lyre — a 6-string lyre played in the evenings for personal music-making and small ceremonies
Kingdom praise songs — each kingdom had specific compositions honouring its royal lineage and historical events
